title: Summer berry pavlova
subtitle: Large meringue with a soft marshmallow centre, topped with whipped cream and fresh summer berries.
excerpt: A pavlova is a large meringue disc with a crisp shell and a soft, chewy marshmallow interior. It holds its shape for an hour after assembly, which makes it a practical dessert for gatherings. Topped with whipped cream and whatever summer berries are best at the market, it is one of the most straightforward impressive desserts you can make.
sourceNotes: Pavlova is a meringue dessert associated with both Australia and New Zealand, with both countries claiming its invention in honour of Russian ballerina Anna Pavlova's 1926 tour. Culinary historian Helen Leach found the earliest printed recipes in both countries in the 1930s. The Australian form typically has a flatter shape and crisper texture; the New Zealand version is softer and higher. This recipe follows the New Zealand method with cornflour and vinegar added to the meringue, which produces a marshmallow centre. The proportions are consistent with those used by Australian food writer Stephanie Alexander in 'The Cook's Companion'.

content.text: disc baked at low heat for over an hour, then left in the oven to cool with the door ajar. The shell is crisp; the centre stays soft and marshmallowy because of the cornflour and vinegar added before baking. The cream and berries go on at the last minute.

The bowl and beaters must be free of any fat, including egg yolk. A single drop of yolk in the whites will stop them whipping. Wipe the bowl with a cut lemon half before starting if you are not certain.

items.name: Egg whites
items.prepNote: from large eggs, at room temperature

content.text: Heat the oven to 130°C fan (150°C conventional). Line a baking tray with baking paper and draw a 22 cm circle on it as a guide. Flip the paper so the pencil mark is underneath.
Whisk {{egg-whites}} in a clean, dry bowl on medium-high speed until they reach stiff peaks. The mixture should be white and hold a sharp peak when the beater is lifted.

With the mixer running, add {{caster-sugar}} a tablespoon at a time, waiting 10 seconds between additions. The meringue will become very thick, glossy, and white. This takes about 10 minutes. Rub a little between your fingers: it should feel smooth, not gritty. If gritty, continue whisking for 2 more minutes.
Fold in {{cornflour}} and {{white-wine-vinegar}} with a large spoon or spatula. Add {{vanilla-extract}} and fold gently to combine.
Spoon the meringue onto the lined tray within the circle, building up the edges slightly higher than the centre to create a shallow bowl shape for the cream and berries.
Bake for 70 minutes, then turn off the oven and leave the pavlova inside with the door ajar for at least 2 hours, or overnight. It will crack slightly as it cools; this is normal.
Whip {{double-cream}} to soft peaks. Spread over the top of the cooled meringue. Pile on {{strawberries}}, {{raspberries}}, and {{blueberries}}. Serve within an hour.

attrs.intro: Pavlova failures usually trace back to the egg whites or the cooling process.

items.fix: Always test by rubbing meringue between your fingers; it should feel smooth. Always leave in the switched-off oven for the full cooling period.
items.cause: Undissolved sugar or meringue removed from the oven too quickly.
items.symptom: Meringue wept and collapsed after baking.

items.fix: Wipe the bowl and beaters with white wine vinegar on a kitchen paper before starting. Any trace of fat prevents aeration.
items.cause: Grease in the bowl, on the beaters, or from a dropped egg yolk.
items.symptom: Egg whites would not whip to stiff peaks.

items.fix: The low, slow bake (130°C fan for 70 minutes) is what keeps the interior soft. If the oven runs hot, reduce to 120°C fan and extend to 80 to 90 minutes.
items.cause: Oven too hot, or baked for too long at too high a temperature.
items.symptom: Pavlova very flat with no marshmallow centre.

attrs.heading: Troubleshooting
